description: Figure 1 presents the mechanical properties of two low-alloy ultrahigh-strength steels, 0Al and 1.3Al (containing ~1.3 wt% Al), in the as-water-quenched (WQ) condition and after tempering at 180 °C for 2 h. Subfigure (a) displays the engineering stress–strain curves, showing that both steels possess high ultimate tensile strength (UTS) in the as-quenched state—reaching approximately 2294 MPa for 0Al and 2145 MPa for 1.3Al—but with limited ductility, especially for 1.3Al which fractured before reaching its maximum load. After tempering, the UTS of both steels decreases to below 2000 MPa (1925 MPa for 0Al and 1876 MPa for 1.3Al), while the total elongation significantly improves to about 13.5–13.7 %. Subfigure (b) summarizes the Charpy impact-absorbing energy (KU₂), revealing a dramatic toughness enhancement upon tempering: the 1.3Al steel rises from 23 ± 2 J to 80 ± 3 J (~3.5× increase), surpassing the tempered 0Al steel (65 ± 3 J). These results demonstrate that the 180 °C tempering treatment achieves an exceptional combination of ~1.9 GPa tensile strength and markedly improved toughness in the 1.3Al steel, supporting the central thesis that high-density coherent NiAl clusters provide effective toughening without severely compromising strength.
